does: This paper introduces RLMT (RL with Model-rewarded Thinking), a training approach that combines chain-of-thought reasoning generation with reinforcement learning to improve language model performance on open-ended tasks. The method requires models to generate extended reasoning before producing responses, then optimizes them using online RL with a preference-based reward model (similar to RLHF). The authors demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple model scales (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B) and optimization algorithms (DPO, PPO, GRPO), achieving 3-7 point gains on chat benchmarks including AlpacaEval2, WildBench, and ArenaHardV2, along with improvements on creative writing and knowledge tasks.
connects: This work extends the paradigm of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which previously showed success in constrained domains like mathematics and code where ground-truth verification is possible. The paper responds to RLVR's limitation of poor generalization to open-ended reasoning tasks by adapting the core insight—that RL can improve reasoning—to use model-based rewards rather than rule-based ones. It builds on established techniques in RLHF and chain-of-thought prompting, combining these existing approaches to address a gap where previous RLVR methods could not effectively handle tasks requiring subjective human judgment.
enables: RLMT establishes a pathway for scaling reasoning-focused training beyond specialized domains to general-purpose dialogue and open-ended tasks. This approach enables future work on developing more efficient preference models as reward signals for RL training, exploring how extended internal reasoning translates to better external performance across diverse task categories. The consistent improvements across model architectures and optimization algorithms suggest broader applicability for enhancing reasoning capabilities in production language models, potentially supporting development of systems that reason more thoroughly before responding and could unlock better performance on complex real-world problems requiring subjective reasoning rather than verifiable correctness.
